inputs.userGoal: Create a real-time document Q&A API leveraging Azure OpenAI and Azure Cosmos DB, expose endpoints using Azure API Management, and enforce rate limits per user.

architecturePrinciples: Secure by default with managed identities
Per-user rate limiting at the API gateway

reasoning.whyChosen: Centralized log sink required by Application Insights and recommended by WAF Operational Excellence pillar for diagnostics and monitoring.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Workspace-based App Insights is the current default; adds minor cost for log ingestion but enables KQL cross-queries.

resources.name: appi-docqa-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Application Insights provides end-to-end tracing across App Service, APIM, and Azure OpenAI calls. Recommended by WAF Operational Excellence pillar.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Native integration is simplest; third-party tools offer broader multi-cloud support but add cost and complexity.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Application Insights requires a Log Analytics workspace as its backing store.

reasoning.whyChosen: Azure OpenAI provides managed GPT models for document Q&A with enterprise security, content filtering, and SLA. S0 is the standard production SKU.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Azure OpenAI is fully managed but usage-based pricing can grow; self-hosted gives cost control at scale but requires GPU infrastructure management.

resources.name: oai-docqa-prod-001/gpt-4o

reasoning.whyChosen: GPT-4o is the recommended model for document Q&A: fast, cost-effective, supports 128k context for long documents. 30K TPM is a safe default.
reasoning.tradeoffs: GPT-4o balances quality and speed; gpt-4o-mini is cheaper but may produce lower-quality answers for complex documents.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Model deployment is a child resource of the Azure OpenAI account.

reasoning.whyChosen: Cosmos DB NoSQL API provides low-latency reads for document storage and Q&A history with flexible schema. Serverless keeps costs minimal for variable traffic. Session consistency balances performance and correctness.
alternativesConsidered: Azure Blob Storage + Azure AI Search
reasoning.tradeoffs: Serverless is single-region only and has no shared-throughput databases, but eliminates idle cost. Provisioned throughput is better for sustained high traffic.

resources.name: cosmos-docqa-prod-001/docqadb

reasoning.whyChosen: SQL Database container to hold the documents and Q&A history collections.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Single database is sufficient for this workload.

resources.dependencyReasoning: SQL Database is a child resource of the Cosmos DB account.

resources.name: cosmos-docqa-prod-001/docqadb/documents

reasoning.whyChosen: Container for storing documents and Q&A history, partitioned by userId for efficient per-user queries and data isolation.
reasoning.tradeoffs: userId partition key optimizes per-user queries; very large users could create hot partitions, but acceptable for typical Q&A workloads.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Container is a child of the SQL Database.

reasoning.whyChosen: Basic B1 Linux plan is the minimum production-grade tier supporting always-on and custom domains. Keeps cost low for an initial deployment.
reasoning.tradeoffs: B1 lacks autoscale and deployment slots; upgrade to S1 or P1v3 when traffic grows or staging environments are needed.

resources.name: app-docqa-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: App Service hosts the Q&A API backend with managed scaling, built-in auth support, and easy deployment. Python 3.12 is the most common runtime for OpenAI SDK workloads.
reasoning.tradeoffs: App Service provides simpler deployment; Functions would be cheaper at very low traffic but adds cold-start latency. Container Apps suits containerized microservices but is more complex for a single API.

resources.dependencyReasoning: App Service needs the plan, connects to OpenAI and Cosmos DB at runtime, and sends telemetry to Application Insights.

resources.name: apim-docqa-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: Consumption tier APIM provides serverless API gateway with pay-per-call pricing, built-in rate limiting policies, and subscription key management for per-user throttling. Ideal default for new APIs.
reasoning.tradeoffs: Consumption tier has cold-start latency (~1s on first call) and limited policy features vs. dedicated tiers. Upgrade to StandardV2 for production SLA and VNet integration.

resources.dependencyReasoning: APIM sits in front of the App Service backend and routes traffic to it.

overallReasoning.summary: This architecture pairs Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o) with Cosmos DB (serverless NoSQL) behind an App Service API, fronted by API Management for gateway controls. APIM enforces per-user rate limits via rate-limit-by-key policies keyed on subscription ID. Cosmos DB serverless eliminates idle costs, and the Consumption APIM tier keeps gateway costs proportional to usage. Application Insights + Log Analytics provide end-to-end observability.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: Serverless Cosmos DB is single-region and has throughput burst limits (5000 RU/s per partition). Consumption APIM has cold-start latency and no SLA. B1 App Service lacks autoscale. These are acceptable defaults for initial deployment; upgrade paths are documented per resource.

plan.validation: All resources deploy to eastus. Cosmos DB serverless is single-region (matches single-location config). APIM Consumption tier requires no VNet. App Service B1 supports always-on. OpenAI S0 supports GPT-4o deployments. No conflicting pairing constraints detected.
